reasoning: The 20-year constant maturity inflation-indexed Treasury yield acts as a highly liquid financial asset, closely following a random walk model in the short term. Any movements up or down essentially represent a coin flip (~50%). The last available data point before Jan 18 is 2.35% (from Friday, Jan 16). Because bond yields are quoted in discrete increments (e.g., to the basis point), there is a small chance (around 3-5%) of a tie, which would push the probability of an "increase" below 0.50. However, the slightly higher recent 1-month average (~2.36%) and recent spikes in long-term nominal yields (such as the 10-year climbing back above 4.2%) introduce a very mild upward short-term expectation (mean reversion to the recent average). Accordingly, the likelihood of an increase is rated slightly above 50% for the near-term Jan 25 resolution date (0.52), and reverts progressively closer to flat randomness (0.51) for the longer-term Feb 17 date, reflecting greater timeline uncertainty.
